summary: Research OpenAI Build Week rules, judging, tech, and winning patterns

findings.summary: The OpenAI Build Week hackathon (https://openai.devpost.com/) runs its Submission Period from July 13, 2026 (9:00 AM PT) to July 21, 2026 (5:00 PM PT) — meaning the period opens TODAY. Judging runs July 22 – August 5, with winners announced on or around August 12, 2026 (2:00 PM PT). Pre-existing work IS allowed with conditions: projects must be "either newly created during the Hackathon Submission Period or, if the Project existed prior to the Submission Period, must have been meaningfully extended using Codex and/or GPT-5.6 after the Submission Period start date." Entrants with pre-existing projects must provide clear documentation distinguishing prior from new work (timestamped Codex session logs, dated commit history), and pre-existing projects are evaluated ONLY on work added during the Submission Period. No maximum team size is stated anywhere in the rules; solo entry is permitted ("Eligible Individuals," "Teams of Eligible Individuals," and "Organizations" may enter; an individual may even join more than one team). Multiple submissions per entrant are allowed if each is "unique and substantially different"; each project wins at most one prize. Eligibility: residents of countries supporting OpenAI API access (Germany is on the list at developers.openai.com/api/docs/supported-countries); excluded are Brazil, Quebec, Russia, Crimea, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and OFAC-designated countries. Employee ineligibility applies ONLY to OpenAI OpCo LLC (Sponsor) and Devpost Inc. (Administrator) plus affiliates and their households — a Deutsche Telekom employee in Germany is eligible, and no employer-consent clause exists in the rules (though German employment/IP law and Telekom internal policy remain a personal due-diligence item). IP: submissions remain the entrant's property; Sponsor gets only a non-exclusive license for judging plus 3-year promotional rights to names/likenesses. Open source use is allowed if licenses are complied with and the submission "enhances and builds upon" the underlying OSS. Submission requirements: working project built with Codex and GPT-5.6 (both required per "Build any project with Codex and GPT 5.6"); category/track selection; text description; <3-minute public YouTube demo video with audio covering what you built and how you used Codex and GPT-5.6 (judges stop at 3 minutes; no unlicensed trademarks/music); repo URL (public with license, or private shared with testing@devpost.com and build-week-event@openai.com); README describing how you collaborated with Codex; the "/feedback Codex Session ID" for the project thread where the majority of core functionality was built; and for Dev Tools/plugins, installation instructions plus a way for judges to test without rebuilding (demo instance, sandbox, test account). Private sites need login credentials in testing instructions. All materials must be in English or include translation. Judging is two-stage: Stage One is pass/fail viability (fits theme, reasonably applies required APIs/SDKs); Stage Two scores four EQUALLY WEIGHTED criteria — Technological Implementation (how thoroughly/skillfully Codex is used), Design (complete, coherent product experience), Potential Impact (credible, specific case for a real problem), Quality of Idea (creativity/novelty/differentiation). Ties break by highest score on the first applicable criterion (Technological Implementation first). Judges are Sponsor-selected, possibly OpenAI employees or third parties. Prizes: $100,000 pool; per track 1st = $15,000 + 2 Dev Day passes + OpenAI promotion + team meeting + 1-year Pro account; 2nd = $10,000 + promotion + Pro account. $100 Codex credits available via form; credit request deadline July 17, 12:00 PM PT. Winners must pass verification (affidavit, W-9/W-8BEN tax forms).

implications_for_winning: The submission period opened today (July 13, 9AM PT) — start all Codex/GPT-5.6 work now so session logs and commit timestamps fall inside the period; keep a clean, dated commit history from day one as it doubles as the required evidence of in-period work.

You can build on a pre-existing codebase, but judges will score ONLY the work added after July 13 — for maximum scoring surface, a fresh project (or a dramatic, well-documented extension) is strategically safer than a light retrofit.

Do the majority of core functionality in ONE Codex thread (or a clearly primary one), since you must submit the '/feedback Codex Session ID' for the thread where the majority of core functionality was built — plan your Codex workflow around this from the start rather than scattering work across many sessions.

Technological Implementation is judged specifically on 'how thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex' AND it is the first tie-breaker criterion — showcase advanced Codex usage (multi-step agentic sessions, tests generated, refactors) explicitly in the README and video, not just the end product.

As a Deutsche Telekom employee in Germany you are eligible (Germany is API-supported; only OpenAI/Devpost employees are excluded), and you retain full IP ownership — but check Telekom internal policy/German employment law on side projects yourself, since the rules are silent on employer consent.
Solo entry is allowed and there is no team size cap; you may also submit multiple substantially different projects across different tracks to increase odds, since each project can win one prize.

Stage One is pass/fail on theme fit and use of the required tools — make the use of BOTH Codex and GPT-5.6 unmistakable in the video, description, and README to avoid silent elimination before scoring.

Design criterion rewards a 'complete, coherent product experience' beyond proof-of-concept — polish UX, error states, and onboarding; judges must be able to run it, so provide a hosted demo/sandbox with credentials and idiot-proof testing instructions (mandatory for Dev Tools track).

Keep the demo video under 3 minutes with audio narration covering what you built and how Codex+GPT-5.6 were used; judges stop watching at 3:00, so front-load the working demo and avoid copyrighted music/trademarks.
Request the $100 Codex credits before Friday July 17, 12:00 PM PT — that deadline is 4 days away and earlier than the submission deadline.

Potential Impact wants a 'credible, specific case for solving a real problem' — per organizer guidance, 'start with the problem, not the model'; pick a narrow real audience and quantify the pain in the description.

License the public repo properly (or share a private repo with testing@devpost.com and build-week-event@openai.com), write all materials in English, and make the app 'function as depicted in the video' — discrepancies are grounds for failure at verification/testing.
open_questions: The rules never define a numeric threshold for 'meaningfully extended' or 'substantially different' — both are at Sponsor/Devpost sole discretion; worth asking in Discord #hackathon-announcements or office hours if planning to extend an existing project.

No explicit maximum team size was found in any fetched section of the rules — apparently unlimited, but confirming with organizers (or the Devpost FAQ tab) would remove residual doubt since Devpost hackathons commonly cap teams.

The exact mechanics of obtaining the '/feedback Codex Session ID' (where the command lives in the Codex UI, what the ID looks like) are not documented on the rules or resources pages — likely covered in Codex docs at https://learn.chatgpt.com/docs/quickstart or Discord.

Whether GPT-5.6 must be used inside the shipped product (as the app's runtime model) or merely as the model powering Codex during development is not explicitly disambiguated ('Build any project with Codex and GPT 5.6'); safest is both, but organizer clarification would help.

Deutsche Telekom internal approval / German employee-invention law (Arbeitnehmererfindungsgesetz) implications for personal ownership of a side project are outside the hackathon rules and were not researched — the entrant should verify with their employer.
Whether submitting the same project simultaneously to other hackathons is permitted is not addressed in the rules (no restriction found, but also no explicit allowance).

findings.summary:
OpenAI Build Week (https://openai.devpost.com/) is a global online hackathon running July 13–21, 2026 (submissions close July 21, 5:00 PM PDT; judging July 22–Aug 5; winners ~Aug 12). $100,000 total across 4 tracks (Apps for Your Life, Work & Productivity, Developer Tools, Education): 1st per track $15,000 + up to 2 Dev Day passes + OpenAI promotion + meeting with the Codex team + 1-year Pro account; 2nd per track $10,000 + promotion + Pro account. ~8,490 participants are registered; the project gallery (/project-gallery and /submissions) is NOT yet published ("The hackathon managers haven't published this gallery yet"), so zero submissions are visible and per-track competition can't be gauged yet — submissions only opened today (July 13).

Judging is two-stage: Stage One is pass/fail ("does the Project reasonably fit the theme and reasonably apply the required APIs" — i.e., Codex AND GPT-5.6). Stage Two has four equally weighted criteria (verbatim): "Technological Implementation: How thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex?"; "Design: Does the project deliver a working or runnable project that has a complete, coherent product experience?"; "Potential Impact: Does the project make a credible, specific case for solving a real problem?"; "Quality of the Idea: How creative and novel is the concept?". Ties break on the FIRST criterion (Codex proficiency) first — making deep Codex usage the single highest-leverage axis.

Submission checklist: built with Codex + GPT-5.6; pick one category; text description of features and how Codex/GPT-5.6 were used; <3-minute public YouTube demo video with audio; code repo (public with license, or private shared with testing@devpost.com and build-week-event@openai.com); README that must "describe how you collaborated with Codex throughout the project"; a Codex Session ID for the thread where core functionality was built; free, unrestricted testing access (demo/site/build) until judging ends; install instructions for plugins/dev tools. Projects must be newly created during the submission period or "meaningfully extended" after July 13 with "clear documentation distinguishing prior work from new work."

Resources: $100 in free Codex credits via a request form, deadline Friday July 17, 12:00 PM PT (overage billed to you); GPT-5.6 background (openai.com/index/gpt-5-6/), model-choice docs, Codex quickstart; a Devpost Hackathons plugin for ChatGPT; OpenAI Discord channels #build-week-chat, #hackathon-announcements, #office-hours; 60+ Codex Ambassador events worldwide (Luma calendar cal-FhWvOxHV0AGL38z: Delhi, Manila, Dubai, Hyderabad, London, Madrid, Nairobi, Buenos Aires, etc.); kickoff livestream July 13 10 AM PT plus live sessions ~daily (July 14 11am, 15 10am, 16 12pm, 17 8:30pm, 20 10am & 5pm, 21 8:30am PDT), daily Discord office hours, and OpenAI Academy sessions (full lineup at openai.com/build-week/, which blocks scraping). Organizer hints on the resources page: "Start with the problem, not the model"; record the demo with voiceover explaining Codex/GPT-5.6 use; keep the repo testable; monitor credit usage.

Judges: Thibault Sottiaux (Head of Product & Platform; ex-Codex engineering lead, now runs OpenAI core product/platform), Kath Korevec (Codex PM; ex-GitHub, Google, Vercel, Heroku), Tara Seshan (product staff; ex-Stripe Head of Product Billing/Treasury, ex-Watershed Head of Product, Thiel Fellow), Leah Belsky (VP Education; ex-Coursera CRO), Peter Steinberger (MTS "Clawfather"; OpenClaw creator, joined OpenAI Feb 2026, builds agent/multi-agent systems across Codex). The panel is heavily Codex-product-centric.

implications_for_winning: Codex proficiency is the kingmaker: it is the first judging criterion AND the tiebreaker. The required Codex Session ID means judges can inspect how you actually drove Codex — plan for a showcase-quality session (multi-step agentic tasks, code review, AGENTS.md, cloud + CLI usage), not a thin wrapper. Document Codex collaboration explicitly in the README and narrate it in the video voiceover.

Stage One is a hard gate: the project must visibly use BOTH Codex (as the build tool) and GPT-5.6 (in the product) and fit a track — a brilliant project that doesn't 'reasonably apply the required APIs' is eliminated before scoring.

The judge panel is Codex product leadership (Sottiaux, Korevec, Steinberger). For the Developer Tools track especially, expect taste for polished, agentic, scrappy-but-real tools (Steinberger built OpenClaw); a tool that improves agent-driven development workflows plays directly to their interests.

'Design' means a complete, runnable product experience — ship something a judge can actually run with zero friction, and keep the demo/test access free and working through Aug 5 (judging end). Broken setup = lost criterion.

'Potential Impact' rewards a credible, specific problem case — matching the organizers' explicit hint 'Start with the problem, not the model.' Anchor the pitch in a concrete user and workflow (e.g., a real enterprise/productivity pain), with specifics, not generic AI-can-do-anything claims.

Logistics that cost wins if missed: request the $100 Codex credits before Friday July 17, 12:00 PM PT; keep the demo video under 3 minutes, public on YouTube, with audio; share private repos with testing@devpost.com and build-week-event@openai.com; if extending prior work, clearly document what was built after July 13.

Competition is large (~8,500 registrants) but there are 8 prizes across 4 tracks; the gallery is unpublished so track crowding is unknown — pick the track where your Codex-usage story is strongest rather than guessing at less-crowded tracks. Education has a dedicated judge (Belsky, ex-Coursera), so education entries need genuine pedagogy credibility.

Use the week's support surface for signal: kickoff livestream (July 13, 10 AM PT), daily Discord office hours (#office-hours), and OpenAI Academy sessions may reveal what organizers emphasize; the Devpost ChatGPT plugin and ambassador events are additional channels to sanity-check your submission against expectations.
open_questions: How many submissions per track will there be? The gallery is unpublished and no counts are visible; re-check https://openai.devpost.com/project-gallery closer to the deadline.
What exactly counts as the 'Codex Session ID' (cloud session link vs. CLI session identifier) and how deeply will judges audit the session transcript?

The full virtual programming lineup (exact office-hours/Academy session times and speakers) sits on https://openai.com/build-week/, which returns 403 to scrapers — needs a browser check or Discord #hackathon-announcements for exact times.
Is there a maximum team size? The official rules specify none.
Do judges score all four criteria per track themselves, or is there per-track judge assignment (e.g., Belsky only on Education)? Not stated in the rules.

findings.summary:
OpenAI Build Week (openai.devpost.com) is a $100,000 online hackathon ending July 21, 2026 5PM PDT, requiring projects "built with Codex and GPT-5.6" that are new or meaningfully extended after the submission period start. Four tracks (Apps for Your Life; Work & Productivity; Developer Tools; Education) each award 1st ($15k + DevDay/Exchange passes + Codex team meeting + 1-yr Pro) and 2nd ($10k + 1-yr Pro). Submissions need: working project, <3-min YouTube demo with audio explaining Codex/GPT-5.6 usage, repo with README describing "how you collaborated with Codex" and "where Codex accelerated your workflow," and a "/feedback Codex Session ID for your Project thread where the majority of core functionality was built." Judging: Technological Implementation ("How thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex?"), Design (complete, coherent product experience, not a proof of concept), Potential Impact (credible, specific case for a real problem/audience), Quality of the Idea (creativity/novelty). Judges include Thibault Sottiaux (Codex product head), Kath Korevec, Tara Seshan, Leah Belsky, Peter Steinberger. Free Codex credits must be requested by July 17, 12PM PT; ~8,475 participants.

GPT-5.6 (limited preview June 26, GA July 9, 2026) is a three-tier family: gpt-5.6-sol (flagship, $5/$30 per 1M tokens; `gpt-5.6` aliases to it), gpt-5.6-terra ($2.50/$15), gpt-5.6-luna ($1/$6). ~1M-token context (reported 1,050,000: up to 922k input/128k output), text+image input, knowledge cutoff Feb 2026. Sol leads coding (Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index 80, beating external-vendor's Fable 5 by 2.8 with less than half the output tokens; "54% more token-efficient" per Altman; TerminalBench 2.1 Sol Ultra 91.9%) and is OpenAI's "strongest cybersecurity model." Marquee API features (Responses API): Programmatic Tool Calling (model writes JS orchestrating tools in an isolated no-network V8 runtime), persisted reasoning (`reasoning.context`: auto/all_turns/current_turn), new `max` reasoning effort, Pro mode (`reasoning.mode:"pro"`), explicit prompt-caching controls (writes 1.25x, reads -90%), and Multi-agent orchestration beta (root agent spawns parallel subagents via hosted actions spawn_agent/send_message/followup_task/wait_agent/interrupt_agent/list_agents; GPT-5.6 only; default 3 concurrent; WebSocket recommended). Realtime voice: gpt-realtime plus GPT-Realtime-2.1/mini (May 2026); consumer full-duplex GPT-Live launched July 8, 2026.

Codex in July 2026 is a multi-surface agent: open-source Rust CLI, IDE extension (VS Code/Cursor/Windsurf/JetBrains), Codex desktop app (macOS/Windows, joined ChatGPT desktop July 9), cloud tasks, GitHub PR-review bot, iOS/mobile with Remote Control GA. Depth features: AGENTS.md (nested + AGENTS.override.md), config.toml, MCP, Skills (SKILL.md in .agents/skills), plugins (@plugin-creator), Automations (scheduled background worktree runs), parallel agents via git worktrees, /review code review, /plan, /fork, /compact, /resume, in-app browser, Computer Use (Mac+Windows), Appshots, Goal mode GA, Sites (deploy/host web apps, preview). The /feedback slash command, typed in the composer, optionally attaches the current session and returns a shareable Session ID; /status also shows the session ID, and sessions live in ~/.codex/sessions (resumable via `codex resume <id>`). The Agents SDK (Python/TS; agents, handoffs, guardrails, sessions, tracing; 2026 additions: configurable memory, sandboxed shell/filesystem tools, built-in MCP) sits atop the Responses API, which is the recommended primitive.

GPT-5.6 context: ~1M tokens (reported 1,050,000 with up to 922k input / 128k output); text+image input; knowledge cutoff Feb 2026 — https://openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-5.6-sol

GPT-5.6 Sol benchmarks: 80 on Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index (+2.8 over external-vendor Fable 5 with <half the output tokens), '54% more token-efficient' (Altman), TerminalBench 2.1 Sol Ultra 91.9%, described as OpenAI's strongest cybersecurity model — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GPT-5.6 and https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/09/openai-launches-its-new-family-of-models-with-gpt-5-6/

New GPT-5.6 API features: Programmatic Tool Calling (model writes JavaScript orchestrating multiple tool calls in an isolated, network-less hosted V8 runtime), persisted reasoning via reasoning.context (auto/all_turns/current_turn), new 'max' reasoning effort, Pro mode (reasoning.mode:'pro'), explicit prompt caching controls — https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model and https://apidog.com/blog/gpt-5-6-programmatic-tool-calling/

Multi-agent orchestration is a Responses API beta, GPT-5.6-only: root agent coordinates parallel subagents via hosted actions spawn_agent, send_message, followup_task, wait_agent, interrupt_agent, list_agents; max_concurrent_subagents default 3; WebSocket transport recommended — https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/responses-multi-agent

Codex is a multi-surface coding agent: open-source Rust CLI (~67k GitHub stars), IDE extension (VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf, JetBrains), desktop app, cloud tasks, GitHub PR review bot, iOS; Codex joined the ChatGPT desktop app on July 9 2026 with multi-repo projects and GPT-5.6 computer-use gains — https://developers.openai.com/codex/changelog (learn.chatgpt.com/docs/changelog)

Codex depth features: AGENTS.md custom instructions (nested per-directory, AGENTS.override.md priority), MCP support, Skills (SKILL.md in ~/.agents/skills or repo .agents/skills), plugins + @plugin-creator, Automations (scheduled tasks in background git worktrees), parallel agents via git worktrees, /review code review (against uncommitted changes, commits, or base branch), Sites deploy/hosting preview, Computer Use on Mac+Windows, Goal mode GA, Remote control GA — https://developers.openai.com/codex/changelog and https://developers.openai.com/codex/learn/best-practices (learn.chatgpt.com/guides/best-practices)

/feedback: typed into the Codex composer; when triggered in an existing conversation you can attach the session, and after submitting 'you'll receive a session ID that you can share'; /status also displays the session ID; sessions stored in ~/.codex/sessions (resume with codex resume <SESSION_ID>) — https://developers.openai.com/codex/app/troubleshooting (learn.chatgpt.com/docs/reference/troubleshooting) and https://github.com/openai/codex/issues/8923

Official Codex best practices: prompt with goal/context/constraints/completion-criteria; durable rules in AGENTS.md; match reasoning effort to task; MCP for external context; package repeat workflows as skills; schedule stable workflows as automations; integrate /review, /plan, /fork, /compact; use subagents for bounded work; OpenAI runs Codex review on 100% of its own PRs — https://developers.openai.com/codex/learn/best-practices

Agents SDK (Python + TypeScript): agents, handoffs, guardrails, sessions, tracing; 2026 evolution added configurable memory, sandbox-aware orchestration, Codex-like filesystem/shell tools, and built-in MCP; Responses API is the underlying primitive and preferred when you want to own the loop — https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents and https://openai.com/index/the-next-evolution-of-the-agents-sdk/

Voice stack July 2026: gpt-realtime speech-to-speech plus GPT-Realtime-2.1 and 2.1-mini realtime reasoning models (May 11 2026); consumer full-duplex GPT-Live launched July 8 2026 and upgrades to GPT-5.6 backend as it reaches GA — https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-realtime/ and https://www.ghacks.net/2026/05/11/openai-releases-three-new-realtime-voice-models-for-the-api-with-gpt-5-class-reasoning/

GPT-5.6 is now the preferred model in Microsoft 365 Copilot, and ChatGPT Work (enterprise agent product) launched alongside GPT-5.6 on July 9 2026 — https://openai.com/index/gpt-5-6-preferred-model-microsoft-365-copilot/ and https://9to5mac.com/2026/07/09/openai-announcing-the-next-chapter-for-chatgpt-today-watch-here/
implications_for_winning: The Codex Session ID is evidence, not paperwork: do the majority of core development in ONE Codex project thread, keep that session exemplary (planning via /plan, AGENTS.md-driven guidance, tests, /review passes), then run /feedback at the end to mint the shareable Session ID. Judges can plausibly inspect the transcript, so a clean, skillful session directly feeds the 'Technological Implementation' score.

Demonstrate Codex depth deliberately and document it in the README: repo-root AGENTS.md (+ nested overrides), a custom Skill (SKILL.md) for a repeated workflow, an MCP server connection, an Automation running in a background worktree, parallel worktree agents for independent features, /review before every merge, and cloud tasks or the GitHub review bot. Each item maps to 'how thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex.'

Build GPT-5.6's marquee features into the product itself, not just the process: Programmatic Tool Calling for multi-tool workflows, the Responses API multi-agent beta (spawn_agent fan-out), persisted reasoning for multi-turn agents, max effort/Pro mode for hard steps, and the ~1M context. Using week-old beta features is the clearest way to look state-of-the-art to OpenAI judges.

Design is a separate, equally weighted criterion: ship a complete, coherent product experience (onboarding, polish, error states), not a technical proof of concept. Codex's Sites feature or its improved GPT-5.6 frontend-design judgment can help get there fast.

Cost/efficiency engineering shows proficiency: route high-volume calls to gpt-5.6-luna or terra, reserve sol for hard reasoning, and use the new explicit prompt-caching controls — mention this in the demo video and README.

Logistics: request free Codex credits before July 17 12:00PM PT; the project must be new or meaningfully extended with Codex/GPT-5.6 after July 13; keep the demo video under 3 minutes with audio explicitly narrating where Codex and GPT-5.6 accelerated the work; pick the track with the least competition-to-fit ratio (Education and Developer Tools have the most specific judge alignment — Leah Belsky is VP of Education; Sottiaux/Steinberger are Codex builders).

Potential Impact requires a 'credible, specific case for a real problem for a real audience' — name the user, quantify the pain, and show a real workflow solved end-to-end rather than a broad platform pitch.
open_questions: Exactly where/how Devpost wants the Session ID entered (dedicated form field vs. README text) and whether a cloud-task session ID is acceptable versus a local CLI/app session shared via /feedback.
Whether judges actually replay the session transcript server-side from the ID, or only verify its existence — affects how much to curate the session.
Exact dated snapshot IDs for GPT-5.6 (e.g., gpt-5.6-sol-2026-07-09) and whether 'Sol Ultra' / Pro mode is API-accessible at all tiers or ChatGPT Pro-only.
Whether GPT-5.6 accepts audio input natively or voice must go through gpt-realtime/GPT-Realtime-2.1.
Team size limits and the full eligible-country list (the Devpost page notes exclusions; Germany appears eligible but verify for telekom.de-based teammates).
Whether the Responses API multi-agent beta requires an org allowlist/flag or is open to all API accounts, and its rate limits during the hackathon week.

findings.summary: Research across OpenAI-run and other mega Devpost hackathons shows a consistent winning formula. The Sept 2025 OpenAI Open Model Hackathon (gpt-oss, $30k, 7 categories, ended Sep 11 2025, winners Oct 2 2025) rewarded vivid, demoable creativity: Best Overall went to RoboChef (gpt-oss planning steps executed by an NVIDIA Isaac GR00T robotic arm with live UI), Best in Robotics to "A Printer… for Smell" (natural-language "scent songs" driving hardware), Wildcard to Bota (gpt-oss-20b playing Dota 2 live). In-person OpenAI Codex hackathons rewarded technical depth over flash: Rippletide won (judges: Greg Brockman, Sonya Huang, Thibault Sottiaux, Lenny Rachitsky, Peter Steinberger) with a continuously-running multi-agent research system plus a "decision layer" — "judges appeared to value practical infrastructure over flashy demos"; Yolodex won $10k with a Codex-agent pipeline that auto-builds YOLO training datasets; camfer won the o1 hackathon with an AI CAD tool exploiting o1's distinctive reasoning. The pattern for OpenAI judges: showcase the sponsor model's *differentiating* capability, not a thin wrapper. Across huge non-OpenAI events the winners are overwhelmingly NARROW, polished, single-problem tools with human stories — not platforms: Chrome Built-in AI Challenge 2024 (8,600 participants/650 projects: Mochi accessibility extension, Opale prompt tool) and 2025 (14,000/1,300: Nutshell hands-free browsing, AAC Board AI, Phonaify pronunciation coach — organizers explicitly cited "real-world user need", accessibility, offline function); Google AI Hackathon (15,478 participants; winner Nested, a lifestyle-based neighborhood matcher); AWS PartyRock (7,660/1,200; winner Parable Rhythm, an interactive crime-thriller game whose creator avoided the "feature vortex" and shot a cinematic game-trailer demo video); Gemini API Competition (winners: Jayu assistant, Gaze Link ALS eye-communication, ViddyScribe audio descriptions). Accessibility/impact framing recurs constantly among winners. On process, Devpost's own guidance and repeat winners converge: judges first run eligibility screening (video length/visibility, required tech, accessible URLs) — non-compliant entries die before judging; the ≤3-min video is the primary judging artifact and "first indicator of how much time was invested" (judge Richard), with a cool video contradicted by a light GitHub repo being a credibility killer (judge Karen). Nathan (22-time winner): 60% explaining/40% demoing, ≥15% of total time on submission polish, GIFs/markdown in the description. Oleksandr (won 7 of 15): solve ONE main problem, include an architecture diagram, 5-part video (problem→solution→walkthrough→tech→recap), spend a full day on video/docs, submit days early. JetBrains judges (June 2026): lead with the problem, show something working within ~90 seconds, one flow, cut features, be honest about limitations. Devpost reports only 5–33% of registrants ever submit, so Build Week's ~8,475 registrants likely means roughly 600–1,300 submissions across four tracks (~150–350 per track). For Build Week specifically, criterion #1 is Codex proficiency and the submission requires a /feedback Codex session ID, a README with setup instructions, and a video with audio explaining where Codex+GPT-5.6 accelerated the workflow — meaning the "how we built it with Codex" story is itself a judged deliverable, not garnish. Net verdict: a narrow, genuinely useful, visually demoable tool with an impact angle, an exceptional sub-3-minute video, a real working repo, and a documented, sophisticated Codex workflow is the highest-probability winning shape.
key_facts: OpenAI Build Week (current contest): 8,475+ registered, $100k across 4 tracks (1st $15k + DevDay passes + OpenAI promotion + team meeting + Pro account; 2nd $10k per track); judging = Technological Implementation (how thoroughly/skillfully the project uses Codex), Design (completeness/coherence), Potential Impact, Quality of Idea; submission requires <3-min public YouTube video WITH AUDIO explaining Codex and GPT-5.6 usage, public/shared code repo, README with setup instructions, and a '/feedback Codex Session ID' from development — https://openai.devpost.com/

OpenAI Open Model Hackathon (gpt-oss, Aug 5–Sep 11 2025, $30k+GPUs, co-hosted by Hugging Face/NVIDIA/Ollama/vLLM/LM Studio; categories Best Overall, Robotics, Weirdest Hardware, Local Agent, Useful Fine-tune, Wildcard, For Humanity); winners announced Oct 2 2025 — https://openai.devpost.com/updates/37529-and-the-winners-are (page now 404s after OpenAI reused the openai.devpost.com subdomain for Build Week; winner content recovered via search index) and https://allhackathons.com/hackathon/openai-open-model-hackathon/

gpt-oss hackathon Best Overall (Hugging Face-sponsored): RoboChef — gpt-oss breaks cooking commands into sequential steps executed by an NVIDIA Isaac GR00T robotic arm with live progress UI; Best in Robotics (NVIDIA): 'A Printer… for Smell' — natural-language prompts compiled by the model into a JSON scent recipe/timeline driving hardware; Wildcard (vLLM): Bota — gpt-oss-20b plays live 1v1 Dota 2 from game state with no fine-tuning — search-indexed content of https://openai.devpost.com/updates/37529-and-the-winners-are

OpenAI Codex Hackathon (OpenAI HQ SF, Feb 5 2026): winner Rippletide built a continuously-running multi-agent system for scientific discovery with an explicit 'decision layer' ranking agent outputs via a citation-aware graph; judges were Greg Brockman, Sonya Huang, Thibault Sottiaux, Lenny Rachitsky, Peter Steinberger, who 'appeared to value practical infrastructure over flashy demos' — https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer and https://cerebralvalley.ai/e/openai-codex-hackathon; winners also announced at https://x.com/OpenAIDevs/status/2019902197199434190

Another OpenAI Codex Hackathon 1st place ($10,000): Yolodex, an 'end-to-end game vision factory' using Codex agents to auto-extract and label video frames into YOLO-ready ML datasets; presented to Sam Altman — https://www.createwith.com/tool/chatgpt/updates/openai-hackathon-winners-create-yolodex

camfer won OpenAI's o1 hackathon with an AI CAD/mechanical-engineering tool that exploited o1's deep reasoning (autonomously designing 5 airfoil profiles to spec via first-principles calculation + simulation); showcased at OpenAI DevDay 2024 — https://camfer.dev/blog/winning-o1-hackathon/

AI Tinkerers NYC 'OpenAI Realtime Voice x Reasoning' hackathon winners: Bitter Lessons, Bellhop, and Ensure (one winner: voice agent that calls insurers during medical emergencies to secure approvals and evidence-backed appeals) — https://nyc.aitinkerers.org/hackathons/h_q2ugc3T5gts/results (page 403s; names from search index)

Google Chrome Built-in AI Challenge 2024: 8,600+ participants, 650+ complete projects; winners judged on 'functionality, purpose, content, user experience, and technical execution'; all winners were narrow single-purpose tools: Mochi (adapts web content for ADHD/dyslexia), WP on-device AI assistant, Opale (reusable prompt templates), Orma (browser memory chat), BrowseGraph (in-browser GraphRAG), The Crooked Tankard (AI text adventure) — https://developer.chrome.com/blog/ai-challenge-winners

Google Chrome Built-in AI Challenge 2025: 14,000+ registrations, 1,300+ submissions; winners emphasized accessibility, offline function, 'significant, real-world user need': Nutshell (hands-free browsing for motor disabilities), AAC Board AI (offline communication aid), Phonaify (pronunciation coaching), Pet Shelter App, Mentelo, Trail (offline outdoor navigation) — https://developer.chrome.com/blog/ai-challenge-winners-2025

AWS PartyRock Generative AI Hackathon: 7,660 registrants, 1,200+ submissions; Overall 1st ($20k AWS credits): 'Parable Rhythm – The Interactive Crime Thriller' by Param Birje; 2nd: Faith manga creation tools; 3rd: Arghhhh! Zombie game — https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/congratulations-to-the-partyrock-generative-ai-hackathon-winners/ and https://info.devpost.com/customer-stories/ai-hackathons-on-devpost-2024

Param's stated winning tactics: prototype multiple ideas then pick the one exercising the sponsor platform's full feature set; build MVP then iterate; avoid the 'feature vortex'; make the demo video a cinematic game-trailer instead of the standard template; write a bonus blog post tying the project to Amazon Bedrock — https://info.devpost.com/blog/user-story-param

Google AI Hackathon (Gemini, ~15,478–15,587 participants, biggest Devpost AI hackathon of 2024): overall winner 'Nested' — matches users to city neighborhoods using Google Maps search history; 8 winning projects total — https://googleai.devpost.com/updates/30503-and-the-winner-is and https://info.devpost.com/customer-stories/ai-hackathons-on-devpost-2024

Gemini API Developer Competition winners (grand prize: custom electric DeLorean): Best Overall 'Jayu' (device-integrated personal assistant); Most Impactful 'VITE VERE' (support for cognitive disabilities); Best Android 'Gaze Link' (ALS patients communicate with eyes); Best Web App 'ViddyScribe' (auto audio descriptions for videos) — accessibility/impact projects dominated — https://ai.google.dev/competition

Devpost data: only '5% to 33% of hackathon registrants submit completed builds' — applied to Build Week's ~8,475 registrants this implies roughly 600–1,300 total submissions split across 4 tracks — https://info.devpost.com/customer-stories/openai-open-model-hackathon

Devpost's 5-judge panel advice: judges check requirements compliance FIRST; the demo video is 'our first indicator of how much time was invested'; a slick video paired with a GitHub repo 'lighter on code' destroys credibility; recycled/rebadged projects and template-minimum submissions are rejected; 'storytelling component is huge'; judges reward projects that deeply use sponsor tech because sponsors are watching for exactly that — https://info.devpost.com/blog/hackathon-judging-tips

Devpost demo-video guide: script the 3 minutes in segments (intro, problem, components, live demo, closing); front-load the elevator pitch in the first few seconds because judges review many projects back-to-back; no slide decks; reserve 2–3 hours for recording/upload; upload to YouTube marked 'Not for Kids' and verify visibility — https://info.devpost.com/blog/6-tips-for-making-a-hackathon-demo-video

Nathan (22-time Devpost winner): 'The project description, visuals, and presentation itself are critical'; spend 60% of video explaining / 40% demoing; allocate at least 15% of total hackathon time to polishing the submission; embed GIFs/images and markdown; differentiate on interface (e.g., voice/graphical when everyone else builds chatbots); hardcoded/sample data is acceptable to prove the concept — https://info.devpost.com/blog/user-story-nathan

Oleksandr (won 7 of 15 hackathons): 'the idea and its potential impact are the most important things'; solve ONE main problem with a few supporting features; include a visual architecture diagram; 5-part video structure (data-backed problem, solution, user walkthrough, technical implementation, recap); allocate a full day for video+documentation; submit several days early — https://info.devpost.com/blog/user-story-oleksandr

JetBrains judging-table article (June 2026, from the JetBrains x Codex hackathon): lead with the problem ('get your audience of judges sharing your frustration'); 'You have to be able to show something working within about 90 seconds'; demo ONE flow and cut features that dilute it; pre-fill data/mock API calls so the demo can't fail; be direct about what works and what doesn't — https://blog.jetbrains.com/ai/2026/06/how-to-win-a-hackathon-notes-from-the-judging-table/

Devpost judging mechanics: submissions pass an eligibility screening before any judging (video length and visibility, required tech incorporated, URLs accessible to judges) — 'Even the most brilliant ideas can be lost in the shuffle if they don't meet the hackathon's criteria'; standard criteria are Technological Implementation, Ease of Use, Demonstration, Potential Impact, Idea Quality, Design — https://info.devpost.com/blog/understanding-hackathon-submission-and-judging-criteria

Devpost/Microsoft winners webinar: read every hackathon detail including bonus tracks; video must cover 'the idea and the problem it solves, the key features you built, and future potential'; submit a day or two early; ChatEDU (Microsoft AI Classroom winner, 3,706 participants) 'originally built it for ourselves' — https://info.devpost.com/blog/winning-with-hackathons-webinar and https://info.devpost.com/customer-stories/ai-hackathons-on-devpost-2024
implications_for_winning: Build narrow, ship polished: across every 5,000+ participant online hackathon examined (Chrome x2, Google AI, PartyRock, Gemini competition), winners were single-problem tools with one flawless flow — accessibility aids, focused extensions, one-genre games — not platforms. Broad 'platform' projects only won at in-person OpenAI HQ events with expert judges (Rippletide). For Build Week's four product-oriented tracks, pick one sharply-defined problem in one track and make one end-to-end flow work perfectly.

Treat 'how we used Codex' as a first-class judged deliverable, not garnish: criterion #1 is literally 'how thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex', and the submission demands a /feedback Codex Session ID plus video narration of where Codex accelerated the workflow. Winners of prior OpenAI hackathons (Rippletide, Yolodex, camfer) won by exploiting the sponsor model's differentiating capability (agents, reasoning, tool use) — plan to demonstrate a sophisticated Codex workflow (multi-agent/parallel tasks, code review, custom AGENTS.md, MCP integrations) and document it explicitly in README and video.

The 3-minute video is close to decisive: it's the primary artifact judges use at this scale, the 'first indicator of how much time was invested', and non-compliant videos (too long, private, no audio) get submissions killed in eligibility screening before judging. Budget a full day: script it (problem → solution → live walkthrough → Codex/GPT-5.6 tech story → recap), state the problem and what the app does in the first 15 seconds, show the working product within 90 seconds, roughly 60% explanation / 40% demo, no slide decks, and differentiate stylistically from the template every other entrant uses (Param's cinematic trailer).

Repo must corroborate the video: judges explicitly cross-check — a flashy video with a thin GitHub repo is a stated credibility killer. Ship a real, runnable codebase with a README containing working setup instructions (a stated Build Week requirement), architecture diagram, screenshots/GIFs, and markdown-formatted Devpost description (Nathan allocates 15%+ of total time to this polish).

Choose an idea with a human-impact story: accessibility and 'real-world user need' projects won disproportionately (Mochi, Nutshell, AAC Board AI, Gaze Link, ViddyScribe, VITE VERE, medical-claims voice agent). 'Potential Impact' is one of only four Build Week criteria — a data-backed problem statement plus a credible affected-user story materially raises scores. Building something you personally need (ChatEDU, Param) reads as authentic.

Hit every checklist item early: judging starts with requirements screening, and many entries die there. Submit 1–2 days before July 21 5PM PDT; verify video <3:00, public on YouTube with audio, repo accessible to the listed judge emails if private, category selected, Codex session ID included, install instructions present (mandatory for Developer Tools track).

Field-size math favors serious entries: with ~8,475 registrants and Devpost's 5–33% submission conversion, expect roughly 600–1,300 submissions split over 4 tracks and 8 prizes — perhaps 150–350 rivals per track, most of them low-effort. A complete, polished, requirement-perfect submission is already top-decile; the competition is against the ~20 serious teams per track.

Don'ts distilled from judges: don't build a generic chatbot/wrapper (judges see dozens — differentiate on interface and capability); don't rebadge an existing project or clone something already on the market; don't feature-creep ('feature vortex') — cut anything not in the demo flow; don't over-index on one criterion (pure tech with no design, or pure idea with no code); don't bury the problem statement under tech talk; don't leave the video/docs to the final hours; don't hide limitations — stating honestly what works builds judge trust.

Winning-shape template for Build Week: a narrow, genuinely useful tool in one track + visible GPT-5.6 capability showcase + documented power-user Codex workflow + accessibility/impact angle + cinematic sub-3-min video that shows the product working in the first 90 seconds + a real repo with excellent README + early submission.

Exact judging weights for Build Week's four criteria are not published on the Devpost page — assume equal weighting unless the rules page states otherwise.
Whether Build Week judging includes a Devpost-staff pre-screen/first-round narrowing before the 5 listed OpenAI judges score (typical for 1,000+ submission events, but unconfirmed for this one).
How deeply the submitted '/feedback Codex Session ID' will be inspected (spot-check vs. real workflow review) — affects how much to invest in a clean, impressive Codex session history.
Total submission count for the gpt-oss hackathon (registrations known to be high but exact submissions never published), which would sharpen the Build Week competition estimate.

findings.summary:
The AI-security landscape splits sharply into a saturated zone and a genuinely open zone. The saturated zone is "AI SOC analyst" alert triage: 100+ vendors and 50+ agentic-SOC startups tracked by Omdia, including Dropzone AI (100+ enterprise customers, 85+ integrations, on Gartner's 2026 Hype Cycle), Prophet, Radiant ($21M raised), Simbian, Intezer, Conifers, plus incumbents — Microsoft added five Security Copilot agents at RSAC 2026, CrowdStrike shipped Charlotte AI Agent and Palo Alto XSIAM Autonomous Response in March 2026, SentinelOne has Purple AI. Commercial forensics also has AI copilots: Magnet Copilot (offline chat-with-case-data with citations), Binalyze Fleet AI (multi-agent, rule-generation agent "Blacklight"), Darktrace/Cado cloud forensics. On the open-source side, plain "forensic tool behind MCP" is already commoditized: multiple Velociraptor MCP servers (SOCFortress, mgreen27), at least three Volatility3 MCP servers, FuzzingLabs' mcp-security-hub (38 servers, 300+ tools), cyproxio/mcp-for-security, and Red Hound's "Protocol SIFT" (external-agent Code + SIFT Workstation, full investigations in 14–18 minutes) — SANS even ran a $22K community hackathon (Apr–May 2026) specifically for forensic MCP integrations. So an MCP wrapper alone will fail the "differs from existing concepts" judging criterion.

What every one of these tools is missing — and openly admits — is verifiability and adversarial robustness. Protocol SIFT is "explicitly not validated for courtroom use"; hallucinated IoCs are documented disrupting SOCs; legal AI fabricates citations in 17–33% of responses with 1,345 court incidents logged; academic work (ProvSEEK, ForensicLLM, DFIR-Metric) says LLM forensic output needs traceable, ground-truth-verifiable citations before it's usable. Simultaneously, 2026 courts now demand hash verification, provenance, and chain-of-custody as "the price of admission" for contested digital evidence in the deepfake era. Anti-forensics detection (timestomping, USN gaps, log clearing) is another documented research gap where standard heuristics are "based on fallacies." Regulatory reporting is a third gap: one EU incident triggers DORA 4h + NIS2 24h/72h/1-month + GDPR 72h reports for 160K+ entities, with fines to €10M/2% of turnover, and manual processes are acknowledged as insufficient. Fourth: 47% of SMBs have no IR plan, 88% of SMB breaches involve ransomware, and 75% say they couldn't survive one — no AI "first-48-hours" responder exists for them. Fifth: victim-facing forensics (stalkerware for domestic-abuse survivors, $7.7B elder-fraud losses in 2025) has essentially one dated tool (TinyCheck).

On clichés to avoid: meeting summarizers, résumé analyzers, FAQ/doc chatbots and quiz generators appear on every "hackathon ideas" listicle; AI code review is a ~$420M-ARR market (CodeRabbit on 2M+ repos, Greptile, Graphite acquired by Cursor); ChatPDF-style RAG hit 1M users in week one back in 2023; and GPT-5.6's own July 9 launch bundles "ChatGPT Work" agents, so generic productivity agents compete with OpenAI itself. The February 2026 OpenAI Codex hackathon winner — judged partly by Sottiaux and Steinberger, both Build Week judges — was a continuously running multi-agent system whose distinguishing feature was an evaluation/decision layer that scored and verified outputs rather than just generating them. That pattern (generation + structured verification) maps exactly onto the biggest hole in AI DFIR: a self-verifying, evidence-cited, anti-forensics-aware autonomous investigator would be novel, high-impact, deeply Codex-native (subagents, hooks, MCP), and sits squarely in the judges' demonstrated taste.
key_facts: Build Week judging explicitly asks 'How thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex?' and 'does the project differ from existing concepts?'; pre-existing projects allowed only if 'meaningfully extended' during Jul 13-21 with timestamped Codex session logs/commits (https://openai.devpost.com/rules)

Build Week: $100K pool, $15K/$10K per track, 8,475+ registered, judges include Thibault Sottiaux and Peter Steinberger; submission needs <3-min video, repo, README, Codex session ID from /feedback (https://openai.devpost.com/)

The Feb 2026 OpenAI Codex hackathon (judges incl. implications_for_winning: Do NOT submit a bare 'forensic tool behind MCP' or an alert-triage bot — SOCFortress, FuzzingLabs, Protocol SIFT and a SANS-sponsored hackathon already did the former, and 100+ vendors do the latter; the 'Quality of the Idea' criterion explicitly penalizes resemblance to existing concepts.

The winning differentiator in this space is a VERIFICATION layer, not more tool-calling: an autonomous investigator where every finding carries machine-checkable citations (artifact path + offset + hash + exact reproducible tool command), a coverage audit of what was NOT examined, and an adversarial second-pass verifier subagent. This attacks the field's admitted #1 failure (hallucination / 'not validated for courtroom use') and replicates the exact generation-plus-evaluation-layer pattern that won the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon in front of two of the same judges (Sottiaux, Steinberger).

Five under-served areas where deep DFIR expertise is an unfair advantage: (1) Court-defensible self-verifying forensic agent (novelty: nobody ships verification-first forensics; impact: courts now demand forensic-grade provenance in the deepfake era); (2) Anti-forensics/tampering hunter that reasons about evidence ABSENCE — timestomp drift, USN gaps, log clears (novelty: documented SoK research gap, known-fallacious heuristics; moat: requires NTFS/$SI-vs-$FN/USN internals knowledge); (3) One-incident-to-many-regulators report engine with live deadline clocks (DORA 4h/NIS2 24h/GDPR 72h/SEC 8-K) generating evidence-cited drafts — 160K+ EU entities, EUR 10M fines, perfect 'Work & Productivity' fit and a credible specific audience; (4) SMB/MSP 'first 48 hours' guided responder with forensically sound evidence preservation for the 47% with no IR plan — huge, quantifiable impact story; (5) Victim-facing stalkerware/elder-fraud evidence kit producing law-enforcement-ready packages ('Apps for Your Life') — near-empty competitive field, emotionally powerful 3-minute demo, and safety-aware forensics (don't tip off the abuser) is genuine expertise.

Codex-proficiency scoring: build WITH Codex visibly — subagents (investigator/verifier/devil's-advocate), MCP servers, hooks, AGENTS.md, and GPT-5.6's Max/Ultra multi-agent modes; capture the required Codex session ID via /feedback and keep timestamped session logs, since all work (or the delta over any pre-existing code) must demonstrably occur July 13-21.

If extending an existing forensic MCP codebase, the rules require clear documentation separating prior work from new work — position the pre-existing tool layer as commodity plumbing and the July-built verification/anti-forensics/reporting layer as the submission.

Design criterion demands 'a complete, coherent product experience, not just a technical proof of concept' — ship a real UI (interactive timeline, tamper heatmap, evidence-cited report with click-to-verify hashes), not a terminal transcript; and script the <3-minute video around one dramatic, concrete case (e.g., agent catches a timestomped backdoor AND proves every claim).

Impact criterion rewards 'a credible, specific case for a real audience' — quantify with the stats above (54% DFIR burnout, 47% SMBs without IR plans, EUR 10M NIS2 fines, $7.7B elder fraud) and name the exact user persona in the submission text.

concepts.name: 1. AIRTIGHT — the self-verifying forensic investigator (RECOMMENDED)
concepts.pitch: An autonomous DFIR agent that investigates a disk/memory image end-to-end and refuses to state anything it cannot prove: every finding carries a machine-checkable citation (artifact path + byte offset + SHA-256 + the exact reproducible tool command), an adversarial GPT-5.6 verifier subagent re-derives each claim from raw evidence before it reaches the report, and a coverage auditor discloses exactly what was NOT examined — turning 'AI-assisted forensics' from a hallucination liability into court-defensible work product.
concepts.why_it_wins: TECHNOLOGICAL IMPLEMENTATION: This is the maximal Codex/GPT-5.6 showcase — the product itself runs GPT-5.6's week-old multi-agent orchestration beta (spawn_agent investigator/verifier/devil's-advocate fan-out), Programmatic Tool Calling to script sequences of MaRa SIFT's 300 forensic tools in the hosted V8 runtime, persisted reasoning (reasoning.context) across a multi-hour investigation, max-effort/Pro mode reserved for the verification pass, and sol/terra/luna tiering with explicit prompt caching for cost engineering; the build process uses every Codex depth feature (see showcase list). Using beta features shipped 4 days ago is the clearest way to look state-of-the-art to OpenAI's own judges. DESIGN: An evidence dashboard where every sentence of the report is a click-to-verify link that re-runs the cited command and re-checks the hash live, plus an interactive investigation timeline and coverage map — a complete product experience, not a terminal transcript. POTENTIAL IMPACT: The field's admitted #1 failure — Protocol SIFT is 'explicitly not validated for courtroom use,' hallucinated IoCs demonstrably disrupt SOCs, 1,345 logged court incidents of fabricated AI citations — while 2026 courts now demand hash verification, provenance, and chain-of-custody as 'the price of admission' for contested digital evidence. Named audience: DFIR analysts and expert witnesses (54% burnout, 64% investigation fatigue). QUALITY OF IDEA: Nobody ships verification-first forensics — the dozens of existing forensic MCP wrappers (SOCFortress, FuzzingLabs, Protocol SIFT, three Volatility MCP servers, a whole SANS hackathon of them) are the prior art this explicitly surpasses, and the generation+evaluation-layer pattern is exactly what won the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon in front of two of these same judges. His NTFS/memory-forensics expertise is an unfair moat no generalist team can copy in a week.
concepts.scope_7_days: Fresh public repo; MaRa SIFT consumed as an external MCP dependency (commodity plumbing, clearly documented as pre-existing). Days 1–2: citation engine — a wrapper that forces every MCP tool result into a signed Finding object (claim, artifact path, offset, hash, exact command, tool version) plus the verifier subagent via the Responses API multi-agent beta. Days 3–4: orchestration (Programmatic Tool Calling investigation playbooks; coverage auditor; one meticulously crafted demo case image containing a timestomped backdoor + cleared event logs) and the web dashboard (timeline, click-to-verify, coverage map). Day 5: end-to-end hardening, hosted judge sandbox pre-loaded with the case, evidence-cited PDF/HTML report export. Days 6–7: README/architecture diagram, video, early submission. CUT ruthlessly: no live-endpoint response, no multi-case management, no regulator reporting (mention as roadmap only), Windows artifacts only for the demo case. One flawless flow: 'load image → autonomous investigation → tampering caught → every claim clicked and proven.'
concepts.biggest_risk: Scope explosion — a 'full investigation pipeline' is months of work, and a half-working broad agent loses to a narrow polished one. Mitigation: the demo case is fixed and rehearsed; the verification loop only needs to be bulletproof for the ~15 tool calls that case exercises; everything else is honest 'supported tools' listing. Secondary risk: judges pattern-match it to the existing crowd of 'security MCP agents' — mitigated by making VERIFICATION (not tool-calling) the first word of the pitch and naming Protocol SIFT/court-admissibility as the prior art being beaten.

concepts.name: 2. GHOST HUNTER — the anti-forensics / evidence-tampering detective
concepts.pitch: A GPT-5.6 agent that hunts what attackers erased — reasoning about the ABSENCE of evidence (timestomp drift between $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME, USN journal gaps, event-log clears, prefetch deletion, Sysmon evasion) and rendering a visual 'tamper heatmap' of the disk with a confidence-scored narrative of what the attacker tried to hide.
concepts.why_it_wins: TECHNOLOGICAL IMPLEMENTATION: Cross-artifact absence reasoning is a genuine frontier LLM task — perfect for max-effort reasoning, persisted reasoning context across artifacts, and Programmatic Tool Calling to sweep MFT/USN/log parsers; GPT-5.6 is marketed as OpenAI's 'strongest cybersecurity model,' and this demos why. DESIGN: The tamper heatmap is the most visually arresting artifact in DFIR — instantly legible to a non-forensic judge in a 3-minute video. POTENTIAL IMPACT: Anti-forensics detection is a documented research gap (2026 SoK calls it understudied; standard timestomping heuristics are 'based on fallacies' per inversecos) and every serious intrusion involves log tampering. QUALITY OF IDEA: Highest novelty-per-square-inch of the three — no vendor or open-source MCP project does absence-reasoning, and it requires NTFS-internals knowledge that is his personal moat (MaRa SIFT already exposes af_timestomp_drift, af_usn_gaps, af_event_log_clear primitives to build on).
concepts.scope_7_days: Narrower than #1, comfortably 7 days: Days 1–2: detection orchestrator that cross-correlates MaRa SIFT's existing af_* tools plus MFT/USN/EVTX parsers into tamper hypotheses via GPT-5.6 multi-agent (one hypothesis agent per anti-forensics technique, a synthesis agent scoring them). Days 3–4: craft a demo image with layered tampering (timestomp + USN gap + cleared Security log); build the heatmap UI + attacker-action reconstruction narrative. Day 5: confidence scoring calibrated against a clean baseline image (false-positive story = credibility), judge sandbox. Days 6–7: docs, video, submit.
concepts.biggest_risk: Narrower impact story than #1 — 'catches tampering' serves fewer users than 'makes AI forensics trustworthy,' and a judge may ask 'is this a feature, not a product?' It is also harder to let judges self-serve test meaningfully (they can't assess whether the tampering detection is RIGHT without domain knowledge). Best used as the dramatic centerpiece CASE inside concept #1 rather than standalone — which is exactly the recommended play.

concepts.name: 3. CASCADE — one incident, every regulator, evidence-cited and on the clock
concepts.pitch: Feed it a confirmed incident and it instantly starts the regulatory clocks (DORA 4h, NIS2 24h/72h/1-month, GDPR 72h, SEC 8-K) and drafts each jurisdiction-specific report with every factual assertion cited to actual forensic artifacts — so a CISO under a 4-hour DORA deadline files accurate, evidence-backed notifications instead of panicked guesswork.
concepts.why_it_wins: TECHNOLOGICAL IMPLEMENTATION: Multi-agent fan-out (one subagent per regulator regime), 1M-token context to ingest full incident timelines + regulation texts, structured outputs per regulator schema — solid but less spectacular than #1/#2. DESIGN: Deadline-clock dashboard is inherently dramatic and immediately comprehensible; report diffing across regimes is a clean UX. POTENTIAL IMPACT: The strongest raw impact numbers of the three — 160,000+ EU entities in scope, fines to €10M/2% of global turnover, manual processes officially deemed insufficient, and it is the purest Work & Productivity fit; a German enterprise practitioner pitching EU compliance is maximally credible. QUALITY OF IDEA: Clearly differentiated — no existing tool chains forensic evidence citations into multi-regulator drafts.
concepts.scope_7_days: Days 1–2: regulation knowledge layer (DORA/NIS2/GDPR reporting requirements as structured schemas + deadline state machine). Days 3–4: evidence-ingestion from a forensic case bundle (reuse MaRa SIFT outputs) and per-regulator drafting agents with citation links. Day 5: clock dashboard + judge sandbox with a pre-loaded ransomware scenario. Days 6–7: docs/video/submit. Cut: no actual submission-portal integrations (export only), 3 regimes max.
concepts.biggest_risk: Weakest Codex/GPT-5.6 fireworks of the three — it can look like 'RAG over regulations with templates,' which flirts with the compliance-chatbot cliché and underperforms on the Technological Implementation tiebreaker. Legal-accuracy liability also makes judges nervous ('is this draft actually DORA-compliant?'). Rank it third; harvest its best feature — deadline clocks + evidence-cited exec summary — as a single export screen in concept #1 if time permits.

[0:15–1:30] The one flawless flow, working product on screen by 0:20 (JetBrains judges: something working within ~90 seconds): load the demo image → AIRTIGHT's agents investigate autonomously (show the investigator/verifier/devil's-advocate subagents fanning out live) → it catches the timestomped backdoor AND the cleared logs → then the money shot: click any sentence in the report and watch it re-run the cited Sleuth Kit command, re-verify the SHA-256, and flash green. Then show the coverage panel admitting what was NOT examined — honesty is a stated judge trust-builder. [1:30–2:15] The Codex + GPT-5.6 story, narrated over real screen recordings captured DURING the build week (record everything from day one): the primary Codex session with /plan and /review, the custom SKILL.md wrapping 20 forensic tools, the MCP-inception moment, then the product's GPT-5.6 internals — multi-agent beta, Programmatic Tool Calling, max-effort verification. Name the features explicitly; this segment is Stage-One insurance and feeds criterion #1. [2:15–2:45] Impact + recap: name the audience (DFIR analysts, expert witnesses, EU incident responders), one or two numbers (54% burnout; courts now demand hash-verified provenance), roadmap in one sentence. Production rules: ~60% explaining / 40% demoing (Nathan, 22-time winner), no slide decks, pre-loaded data so the demo cannot fail live, calm human voiceover in English (his practitioner authenticity — 'I investigate breaches for a living' — is a differentiator; do NOT mention Deutsche Telekom or show its branding), no music, upload to YouTube as public, 'Not made for kids,' verify visibility from a logged-out browser, and differentiate stylistically from the default template — shoot it like a case file being cracked, not a screencast.
timeline: JULY 13 (TODAY) — Lock-in day: Request the $100 Codex credits form NOW (deadline is the 17th, do not wait). Watch/skim the 10AM PT kickoff livestream for organizer emphasis. Register on Devpost, join Discord (#build-week-chat, #office-hours) and ask two clarifying questions: how the Session ID field works, and whether GPT-5.6 must be in the product runtime (build as if yes regardless). Create the fresh public repo (MIT/Apache-2.0 license) — AIRTIGHT lives here, MaRa SIFT stays a documented external MCP dependency. Open THE primary Codex thread; write AGENTS.md; run /plan for the architecture; commit the Finding/citation schema (claim + artifact path + offset + SHA-256 + exact command + tool version) before midnight so day one shows real code. Start screen-recording all Codex sessions for video B-roll. Begin the Telekom internal-policy check in parallel.

JULY 14 — Verification core: Build the citation engine — the wrapper forcing every MaRa SIFT MCP tool result into a signed Finding object — using the custom Codex Skill (write SKILL.md first, then let it wrap tools in bulk). Stand up the GPT-5.6 Responses API multi-agent skeleton: root investigator + verifier subagent that re-derives claims from raw evidence and rejects uncited assertions. First /review pass. Nightly-regression Automation created.

JULY 15 — Orchestration + the case: Implement Programmatic Tool Calling investigation playbooks (triage → timeline → anti-forensics sweep → correlation) and the coverage auditor. Craft the demo evidence image with surgical care — a timestomped backdoor, a USN gap, cleared Security logs — this image IS the demo; verify AIRTIGHT catches all three with verified citations. Add the devil's-advocate subagent on max reasoning effort. Model-tier routing (luna/terra/sol) + prompt caching in place.

JULY 16 — Product experience: Spawn a parallel Codex worktree agent to build the web dashboard — investigation timeline, tamper indicators, coverage map, and the click-to-verify interaction (re-run command, re-check hash, green flash). Merge via /review. Wire the evidence-cited HTML/PDF report export. First full end-to-end run: image in → verified report out, untouched by hand.

JULY 17 — Hardening + judge access (credits deadline noon PT — already requested day 1): Error states, onboarding screen, empty states — Design is an equally weighted criterion and 'complete, coherent product experience' is the bar. Deploy the hosted judge sandbox pre-loaded with the demo case (one click, zero setup, credentials in testing instructions); confirm it survives a cold judge visit. Run the clean-baseline image to demonstrate the false-positive story. Fix everything the nightly regression caught.

JULY 18 — Feature freeze + written materials: No new features after today. README 'Built with Codex' chapter (dated commits, session excerpts, AGENTS.md/Skill/Automation inventory, GPT-5.6 feature map, explicit prior-work vs. new-work section for MaRa SIFT), architecture diagram, screenshots/GIFs, Devpost description in polished markdown (problem stats up top: hallucinated IoCs, 1,345 court incidents, 54% burnout). Second full rehearsal of the demo flow. Ask any remaining questions at Discord office hours.

JULY 19 — Video day: Script the 2:45 video in the five-part structure (problem → solution → live walkthrough → Codex/GPT-5.6 story → impact recap), assemble build-week screen recordings, record voiceover, shoot the click-to-verify money shot until it's perfect. Rough cut by end of day.

JULY 20 — SUBMIT (a full day early): Final video edit; upload to YouTube public, verify from logged-out browser, confirm <3:00. Run /feedback in the primary Codex thread to mint the Session ID. Final compliance sweep against the rules checklist: track selected (Work & Productivity), repo public+licensed, README complete, sandbox link + credentials in testing instructions, English throughout, no trademarks/music. Submit on Devpost and confirm receipt. Winners' consensus: submit days early — do not be the person debugging at 4:50 PM PDT.

JULY 21 — Buffer + verification: Re-test the entire submission as a hostile judge (fresh browser, video link, sandbox, repo clone, README instructions). Fix any Devpost form issues before 5:00 PM PDT. Snapshot/backup the sandbox deployment. JULY 22–AUG 5: keep the demo instance alive and monitored through the entire judging window — a dead link on July 30 loses the Design criterion; set an uptime alert.
